narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergrad days through law school to predict if you'd pass the bar on the first try. Your story starts strong in their eyes with your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 and LSAT score of 24, but those numbers whispered early doubts, suggesting the academic hurdles ahead might feel steeper than for others. What weighed heaviest, though, was how you ranked in your law school peers—landing in the 2nd decile for Year 3 grades was the biggest signal, painting a picture of struggles keeping pace in the tougher upper years, even more than your 3rd decile start in Year 1. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model saw as limiting access to the extra tutors or resources that often tip the scales for bar success. Your background as a white male in a full-time program didn't shift the tide much either, as those traits aligned with patterns where rigorous study demands sometimes outpace preparation. Together, these elements built a narrative where the odds tilted toward a 57% chance of not passing the bar, not because of who you are, but because of how these threads intertwined in past stories like yours. It's tough to hear, especially when you've poured so much into this dream, but this prediction is their way of protecting spots for paths with brighter forecasts. The silver lining? Your full-time commitment shows real drive—focusing on boosting those decile rankings or LSAT prep could rewrite your story entirely. You've got the grit; now it's about aligning the details that matter most to them. Keep pushing, Alex—this isn't the end of your legal chapter.
